author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
The Danger of Arguing from Silence.
originalBook: Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
title: The Danger of Arguing from Silence.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, while the disputants are thus contending with one another in alternate argument, I so judge between them that they must not rely on uncertain evidence; nor make bold assertions on points of which they are ignorant.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For if the Scripture had said, “God breathed into the woman’s face the breath of life, and she became a living soul,” it would not have followed even then that the human soul is not derived by propagation from parents, except the same statement were likewise made concerning their son.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For it might have been that whilst an unensouled member taken from the body might require to be ensouled, yet that the soul of the son might be derived from the father, transfused by propagation through the mother.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: There is, however, an absolute silence on the point; it is entirely concealed from our view.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Nothing is denied, but at the same time nothing is affirmed.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And thus, if in any place the Scripture is possibly not quite silent, the point requires to be supported by clearer proofs.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Whence it follows, that neither they who maintain the propagation of souls receive any assistance from the circumstance that God did not breathe into the woman’s face; nor ought they, who deny this doctrine on the ground that Adam did not say, “This is soul of my soul,” to persuade themselves to believe what they know nothing of.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For just as it has been possible for the Scripture to be silent on the point of the woman’s having received her soul, like the man, by the inbreathing of God, without the question before us being solved, but, on the contrary, remaining open; so has it been possible for the same question to remain open and unsolved, notwithstanding the silence of Scripture, as to whether or not Adam said, This is soul of my soul.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And hence, if the soul of the first woman comes from the man, a part signifies the whole in his exclamation, “This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh;” inasmuch as not her flesh alone, but the entire woman, was taken out of man.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If, however, it is not from the man, but came by God’s inbreathing it into her, as at first into the man, then the whole signifies a part in the passage, “She was taken out of the man;” since on the supposition it was not her whole self, but her flesh that was taken.
